SpecificIssue: Default primary buttons have insufficient contrast against background
ExpandedDescription: Primary buttons (.btn-primary) use default Bootstrap blue (#337ab7) on white/light background, resulting in contrast ratio of ~3.8:1 (fails WCAG 1.4.3 AA and 1.4.6 AAA). This affects all call-to-action buttons in portals, making them hard to read for low-vision users.
AcceptanceCriteria: All primary buttons achieve at least 7:1 contrast ratio in normal text and 4.5:1 in large text when tested with WAVE or axe-core.
Test: Load litmus test widget, inspect .btn-primary with Contrast Checker tool in DevTools, confirm ratio >=7:1 and button text remains readable.
Solution1: Add CSS var --sb-primary: #003366 in :root and override .btn-primary { background: var(--sb-primary); color: #fff; }
Solution2: Use SCSS mixin for contrast-boosted buttons in snowboots-core-overrides.scss
Solution3: Apply !important only on .btn-primary in a11y.scss for immediate fix
Solution4: Update Theme CSS Variables with higher contrast color and recompile portal

SpecificIssue: Secondary buttons fail 4.5:1 ratio on hover
ExpandedDescription: Secondary buttons (.btn-default or .btn-secondary) on hover change to light gray with low contrast text (ratio drops to ~3:1), violating WCAG 1.4.3 and making interactive states unusable for color-blind users.
AcceptanceCriteria: Hover state on secondary buttons meets 4.5:1 ratio; no regression in non-hover state.
Test: Hover mouse/keyboard focus over secondary button in litmus widget and use browser contrast tool to verify ratio.
Solution1: Override .btn-default:hover { background: #555; color: #fff; } in core-overrides
Solution2: Introduce --sb-secondary-hover var and use in both files
Solution3: Add focus-visible styles that also boost hover contrast
Solution4: Theme-level variable override for btn-default

ExpandedDescription: Text with .text-muted class uses #777 gray, resulting in ~3.5:1 ratio on white bg, failing readability for all users with visual impairments across labels, help text, and secondary info.
AcceptanceCriteria: All .text-muted instances achieve >=4.5:1 (preferably 7:1) contrast.
Test: Scan page with axe-core; hover over muted text in litmus widget and confirm contrast passes.
Solution1: Redefine .text-muted { color: #555 !important; } in a11y.scss
Solution2: Use CSS var --sb-muted: #555 and apply globally
Solution3: Add @extend or mixin in SCSS for custom muted classes
Solution4: Portal-wide CSS variable injection in Theme

SpecificIssue: Link text in navigation not meeting 4.5:1
ExpandedDescription: Navigation links (navbar, breadcrumbs, sidebar) use default #337ab7 blue on light backgrounds with ratio ~4.2:1 or less, failing WCAG 1.4.3 for links and causing navigation usability issues.
AcceptanceCriteria: All navigation links meet 4.5:1 minimum (7:1 target for AAA).
Test: Tab through navigation in litmus widget and use WAVE tool to flag link contrast.
Solution1: Override .navbar a, .breadcrumb a { color: #004080; }
Solution2: CSS var --sb-link-nav: #004080 with :focus boost
Solution3: Combine with focus-visible rule for dual fix
Solution4: Theme color palette update for links

SpecificIssue: Alert warning text on default background
ExpandedDescription: Warning alerts (.alert-warning) use orange text/background with ratio ~3.9:1, violating WCAG 1.4.3 and making critical warnings hard to read in service catalog and notifications.
AcceptanceCriteria: Warning alert text/background combination achieves >=7:1 contrast.
Test: Trigger alert in litmus widget, inspect with axe, confirm pass.
Solution1: Override .alert-warning { color: #000; background: #ffe699; } in core
Solution2: Dedicated a11y rule with !important for alerts
Solution3: CSS vars for all alert types
Solution4: Theme Builder color sync for alerts

ExpandedDescription: Success alerts (.alert-success) use green text/background with ratio ~3.7:1 on default white, failing WCAG 1.4.3 AA and 1.4.6 AAA. This makes positive feedback messages (order submitted, ticket closed) difficult to read for users with low vision or color vision deficiencies across all service portal notifications and catalog confirmations.
AcceptanceCriteria: All success alert text/background combinations achieve at least 7:1 contrast ratio with no visual regression on non-alert elements.
Test: Trigger a success alert in the litmus test widget (or any catalog confirmation page), open DevTools Contrast Checker or run axe-core, and confirm the ratio passes 7:1 while the alert remains visually distinct.
Solution1: Override .alert-success { color: #000; background-color: #d4edda; } in snowboots-core-overrides.scss
Solution2: Add CSS custom property --sb-alert-success-bg: #c3e6cb and --sb-alert-success-text: #155724 then reference in both core and a11y files
Solution3: Apply a targeted !important rule only in snowboots-a11y.scss for immediate portal-wide enforcement
Solution4: Synchronize with Theme Builder by adding success color variables and recompiling the portal theme

ExpandedDescription: Danger labels and alert-danger use red text (#d9534f) on light backgrounds with ratio ~4.1:1, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and making critical error or delete actions hard to discern for low-vision users in forms, tables, and confirmation dialogs.
AcceptanceCriteria: All danger elements achieve 7:1 contrast; danger text remains distinguishable from success/warning.
Test: Inspect any .label-danger or .alert-danger in the litmus widget with WAVE or axe-core and confirm pass; also test in a real delete confirmation dialog.
Solution1: Set .label-danger, .alert-danger { color: #fff; background: #c9302c; } in core-overrides
Solution2: Define --sb-danger-text and --sb-danger-bg CSS vars with dark background
Solution3: Use a dedicated a11y.scss rule with higher specificity and !important
Solution4: Update portal Theme record CSS Variables for danger states and re-test

ExpandedDescription: Table headers (.table > thead > th) use light gray background (#f5f5f5) with dark text yielding only ~3.9:1 ratio, violating WCAG 1.4.3 in list views, reports, and data tables throughout ServiceNow Service Portal.
AcceptanceCriteria: All table headers achieve at least 7:1 contrast ratio with no loss of zebra striping or readability.
Test: Load a table-heavy page or the litmus widget table section, run axe-core specifically on th elements, and confirm contrast passes.
Solution1: Override thead th { background: #333; color: #fff; } in core-overrides.scss
Solution2: Introduce --sb-table-header-bg and --sb-table-header-text variables and apply globally
Solution3: Add a scoped rule in a11y.scss targeting .table-striped as well
Solution4: Theme-level SCSS variable override for table colors

ExpandedDescription: Form placeholder text (e.g. in .form-control::placeholder) uses very light gray (~#999 or lighter) resulting in 2.8:1 ratio on white input backgrounds, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and making input hints unusable for many users in catalog items and search forms.
AcceptanceCriteria: All form placeholders achieve minimum 4.5:1 contrast (target 7:1) and remain visible when focused.
Test: Focus any input in the litmus widget, type to remove placeholder, then use browser dev tools to inspect ::placeholder and run contrast check.
Solution1: Set input::placeholder { color: #555; } in core-overrides
Solution2: Create --sb-placeholder-color var and use in both SCSS files
Solution3: Add !important placeholder rule in a11y.scss for instant enforcement
Solution4: Extend Theme CSS to include placeholder color variables

ExpandedDescription: Breadcrumb links use default link color with ratio ~4.1:1 on the page background, failing WCAG 1.4.3 for navigation paths and causing orientation issues for users relying on high contrast in long service catalog or knowledge base journeys.
AcceptanceCriteria: All breadcrumb links (including current page) meet 4.5:1 minimum with active item clearly distinguishable.
Test: Navigate to a page with breadcrumbs in the litmus widget or any portal page, tab through links and confirm with WAVE tool that all pass.
Solution1: Override .breadcrumb > li > a { color: #004080; font-weight: bold; } in core
Solution2: Define --sb-breadcrumb-link var with darker shade and apply :visited/:hover
Solution3: Combine with focus-visible rule in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme CSS Variables update for breadcrumb colors

ExpandedDescription: Pagination active page indicators (.pagination > .active > a) use a light blue background with dark text giving only ~3.6:1 ratio, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and making it difficult for users to identify the current page in long lists, knowledge search results, or ticket history tables.
AcceptanceCriteria: All active pagination items achieve at least 7:1 contrast with clear visual distinction from inactive items.
Test: Navigate to a paginated list in the litmus widget or any ServiceNow list view, click through pages, and run axe-core to confirm active state contrast passes.
Solution1: Override .pagination > .active > a { background: #003366; color: #fff; } in core-overrides.scss
Solution2: Introduce --sb-pagination-active-bg and --sb-pagination-active-text CSS vars and reference them
Solution3: Add !important rule in a11y.scss specifically for .pagination .active
Solution4: Update Theme Builder pagination colors and recompile

SpecificIssue: Icon-only buttons with low contrast icons
ExpandedDescription: Icon-only buttons (e.g. glyphicon buttons without text) use default gray icons (#737373) on light backgrounds with ratio ~3.2:1, violating WCAG 1.4.3 and 1.4.11 and making toolbar actions (edit, delete, filter) unusable for low-vision users in forms, lists, and dashboards.
AcceptanceCriteria: All icon-only buttons achieve minimum 4.5:1 icon contrast (7:1 target) and remain distinguishable.
Test: Inspect any glyphicon-only button in the litmus widget with the browser color picker and contrast tool; confirm passes after fix.
Solution1: Set .btn .glyphicon { color: #222; } in core-overrides
Solution2: Define --sb-icon-button-color var and apply to all icon buttons
Solution3: Use SVG filter or stroke for higher contrast icons in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme-level icon color variable injection

ExpandedDescription: Chart legend text and color swatches in dashboard widgets use small gray text and thin colored lines with ratio ~3.1:1, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and 1.4.11 and preventing users from correctly identifying data series in performance, incident, or SLA charts.
AcceptanceCriteria: All chart legend items achieve >=4.5:1 contrast and are clearly readable at 100% zoom.
Test: Open any dashboard widget containing a chart in the litmus test page, run WAVE, and confirm legend text passes contrast.
Solution1: Override .chart-legend { color: #222; font-weight: bold; } in core-overrides
Solution2: Add CSS vars for each chart series color with darker variants
Solution3: Apply high-contrast mode media query in a11y.scss
Solution4: Sync with Theme Builder for widget-specific colors

ExpandedDescription: Progress bars (.progress-bar) show white or light text on colored bars with ratio ~3.4:1 (especially on success/info bars), failing WCAG 1.4.3 and making percentage complete unreadable in task trackers, SLA indicators, and catalog status displays.
AcceptanceCriteria: All progress bar text achieves 7:1 contrast against its background bar color.
Test: Load a progress bar in the litmus widget, inspect the inner text with axe-core, and confirm pass after override.
Solution1: Set .progress-bar { color: #fff !important; } with darker bar backgrounds in core
Solution2: Define --sb-progress-text and --sb-progress-bg vars
Solution3: Use !important only in a11y.scss for progress bars
Solution4: Theme CSS update for progress component colors

ExpandedDescription: Badge and label components (.badge, .label) across all variants (default, primary, success, etc.) use combinations yielding 3.5:1–4.0:1 ratios, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and making status indicators (priority, state, count) hard to read in lists, tickets, and notifications.
AcceptanceCriteria: Every badge/label variant meets minimum 7:1 contrast with no color bleed or regression.
Test: Scan all label/badge examples in the litmus widget with axe-core and confirm all 6 variants pass.
Solution1: Create comprehensive overrides for .label*, .badge* in core-overrides.scss with darker backgrounds
Solution2: Introduce a full set of --sb-badge-* CSS variables
Solution3: Add high-contrast !important block in a11y.scss
Solution4: Extend Theme record with badge color variables

SpecificIssue: Navbar brand text in light theme
ExpandedDescription: Navbar brand/logo text (.navbar-brand) in light themes uses default dark gray (#333) on very light header backgrounds resulting in ~4.3:1 ratio, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and making the portal identity hard to read for low-vision users at the top of every page.
AcceptanceCriteria: All navbar brand text achieves at least 7:1 contrast with no impact on logo images or mobile collapse.
Test: Load any portal page, inspect .navbar-brand with contrast tool, and confirm passes in both light and dark modes.
Solution1: Override .navbar-brand { color: #111; font-weight: 700; } in core-overrides.scss
Solution2: Define --sb-navbar-brand-color var and apply in both files
Solution3: Add high-contrast media query rule in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme Builder navbar color variable update

ExpandedDescription: Footer links in the portal footer use standard link color with ratio ~4.0:1 on footer background, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and causing usability problems for users scrolling to bottom for copyright, privacy, or help links.
AcceptanceCriteria: All footer links meet 4.5:1 minimum (7:1 target) and remain distinguishable when visited.
Test: Scroll to footer in litmus widget or any page, tab through links, and verify with WAVE tool.
Solution1: Override footer a { color: #004080; } in core-overrides
Solution2: Create --sb-footer-link var with darker shade
Solution3: Combine with focus-visible in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme record CSS Variables for footer section

ExpandedDescription: Modal dialog headers (.modal-header .modal-title) use default text color on light header background with ratio ~3.9:1, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and making important dialog titles (confirm delete, edit record) difficult to read during critical workflows.
AcceptanceCriteria: All modal header text achieves 7:1 contrast with no regression to modal body.
Test: Open any modal in the litmus widget or a real form, inspect header text, and confirm contrast passes.
Solution1: Override .modal-header .modal-title { color: #222; } in core
Solution2: Introduce --sb-modal-header-text var
Solution3: Apply !important scoped rule in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme-level modal styling override

ExpandedDescription: Inactive tabs in Bootstrap tab components use gray text on light backgrounds with ratio ~3.8:1, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and making it hard for users to distinguish active vs inactive tabs in multi-tab forms and knowledge articles.
AcceptanceCriteria: All tab states (active/inactive/hover/focus) meet 4.5:1 with clear active distinction.
Test: Click through tabs in the litmus widget, inspect inactive tabs with axe-core, and confirm pass.
Solution1: Override .nav-tabs > li > a { color: #555; } and .nav-tabs > li.active > a { color: #fff; background: #337ab7; } in core
Solution2: CSS vars for --sb-tab-inactive and --sb-tab-active
Solution3: High-contrast focus rule in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme Builder tab component color sync

ExpandedDescription: Tree view nodes in hierarchical lists (e.g. knowledge categories, service catalog folders) use default gray text with ratio ~3.7:1 on white, failing WCAG 1.4.3 and reducing readability in expanded navigation trees.
AcceptanceCriteria: All tree node text achieves minimum 7:1 contrast and remains readable when expanded/collapsed.
Test: Expand a tree in the litmus widget or knowledge base, run contrast check on node labels, and verify.
Solution1: Set .treeview .node { color: #222; } in core-overrides
Solution2: Define --sb-tree-node-color var
Solution3: Add !important in a11y.scss for tree classes
Solution4: Theme CSS Variables for tree components

SpecificIssue: No visible focus ring on buttons
ExpandedDescription: Buttons (.btn and variants) have no visible :focus or :focus-visible outline by default in Bootstrap 3.3.6, violating WCAG 2.4.7 and 2.4.11. Users relying on keyboard navigation cannot see which element has focus, especially in forms, modals, and action bars.
AcceptanceCriteria: Every interactive button shows a clear, high-contrast focus indicator (minimum 3px solid outline with 3:1 contrast) that does not disappear on click or scroll.
Test: Tab through all buttons in the litmus test widget using keyboard only; confirm a visible ring appears on every button and remains until focus moves.
Solution1: Add html body .btn:focus-visible { outline: 4px solid #0066cc; outline-offset: 3px; } in snowboots-core-overrides.scss
Solution2: Define --sb-focus-color and --sb-focus-offset CSS vars and apply globally
Solution3: Create a dedicated high-contrast focus block with !important in snowboots-a11y.scss
Solution4: Add focus styles to Theme record and test across browsers

SpecificIssue: Focus ring obscured by other elements
ExpandedDescription: Focus rings on buttons, inputs, and links are partially or fully hidden behind overlapping elements (z-index issues in widgets, modals, or custom CSS), violating WCAG 2.4.7 and preventing keyboard users from tracking their location.
AcceptanceCriteria: Focus indicators are never obscured; they appear above all other elements with sufficient offset and remain visible in all states.
Test: Open overlapping elements in litmus widget (e.g. modal with buttons), tab through, and visually confirm ring is fully visible and not clipped.
Solution1: Add z-index: 9999; to focus rules in core-overrides
Solution2: Use outline-offset: 4px combined with box-shadow fallback in both files
Solution3: Apply !important + higher specificity in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme-level global focus layer with CSS variables

ExpandedDescription: Default or custom focus outlines are only 1px thin with zero offset, providing poor visibility (fails WCAG 2.4.11 non-text contrast and 2.4.7), especially on small buttons or high-contrast themes.
AcceptanceCriteria: All focus indicators use at least 3px thickness and 3px offset with 3:1 contrast ratio.
Test: Inspect focused element in DevTools on litmus widget and measure outline with ruler tool; confirm passes after fix.
Solution1: Set outline: 3px solid var(--sb-focus); outline-offset: 3px; in core-overrides
Solution2: Introduce --sb-focus-thickness and --sb-focus-offset vars
Solution3: Use box-shadow: 0 0 0 4px rgba(...) as fallback in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme Builder custom focus style injection

ExpandedDescription: Links (<a> tags) have no or very faint focus indicator (default underline only), violating WCAG 2.4.7 and making keyboard navigation through navigation, breadcrumbs, and content links unusable for many users.
AcceptanceCriteria: Every link shows a clear focus ring or bold underline with high contrast when tabbed to.
Test: Tab through all links in the litmus widget and confirm visible indicator on each.
Solution1: Override a:focus-visible { outline: 3px solid #0066cc; } in core
Solution2: CSS var --sb-link-focus with text-decoration boost
Solution3: High-contrast !important rule in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme-level link focus styles

ExpandedDescription: When a modal opens, keyboard focus is not automatically moved to the modal content or close button (common in Bootstrap modals), causing focus to remain on background elements and creating a keyboard trap or disorientation, violating WCAG 2.4.3 and 2.4.7.
AcceptanceCriteria: Focus is programmatically moved to the first focusable element inside the modal on open and trapped until closed.
Test: Open any modal via keyboard in litmus widget or real page, tab inside, and confirm focus starts inside modal with no background interaction.
Solution1: Add small JS snippet to enforce modal focus management (vanilla JS polyfill)
Solution2: Override .modal { } with CSS that enables focus styles + note for JS enhancement in core
Solution3: Dedicated a11y rule + aria-hidden on background in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme/Portal script include for modal focus handler

ExpandedDescription: Bootstrap modals and custom dialogs trap keyboard focus incorrectly (focus can escape to background or get stuck without escape key handling), violating WCAG 2.1.1 and 2.4.3. Users cannot navigate out or within the modal using only keyboard, especially in confirmation or edit dialogs.
AcceptanceCriteria: Modal focus is fully trapped inside the dialog until closed, with Escape key working and focus returned to trigger element.
Test: Open a modal with keyboard, attempt to tab outside (should fail), press Escape (should close), and confirm focus returns correctly.
Solution1: Add vanilla JS focus trap polyfill loaded via Theme JS Include
Solution2: CSS rule to set modal as inert on background + aria-modal=true in core-overrides
Solution3: Dedicated a11y.scss rule with role and aria attributes enforcement
Solution4: Portal script include with jQuery-compatible modal focus handler

ExpandedDescription: Form fields, labels, and buttons have illogical tab order (e.g. labels after inputs or buttons before fields) due to Bootstrap layout or custom widget HTML, violating WCAG 2.4.3 and causing keyboard users to navigate in confusing sequences.
AcceptanceCriteria: Tab order follows logical reading order (left-to-right, top-to-bottom) with no jumps or skips in any form.
Test: Tab through the entire litmus widget form section and any real catalog item; confirm sequential logical order.
Solution1: Add tabindex=0" and reorder HTML comments in widget templates (note in core)"
Solution2: CSS logical properties + flex order override in core-overrides
Solution3: ARIA flow and tabindex fixes in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme-level form template standardization

ExpandedDescription: No visible or accessible skip link at the top of pages to bypass repetitive navigation (navbar, sidebar), violating WCAG 2.4.1 and forcing keyboard users to tab through hundreds of items before reaching content.
AcceptanceCriteria: A visible, focusable skip link appears on first tab and jumps directly to main content.
Test: Press Tab once on page load in litmus widget or any portal page; confirm skip link appears and works.
Solution1: Add Skip to main at top of pages in core-overrides styling"
Solution2: CSS rule to make skip link visible on focus with high contrast
Solution3: Full a11y.scss block with position and styling
Solution4: Portal header widget update with skip link

SpecificIssue: Tab order jumps in dynamic widgets
ExpandedDescription: Dynamic widgets (lists, carousels, AJAX-loaded sections) cause sudden tab order jumps or skipped elements when content loads, violating WCAG 2.4.3 and disorienting keyboard users in service catalog or knowledge base pages.
AcceptanceCriteria: Tab order remains consistent and predictable even after dynamic content loads.
Test: Load a dynamic widget in litmus test, trigger refresh/AJAX, and tab through; confirm no jumps.
Solution1: Add mutation observer JS to re-order tabindex on load
Solution2: CSS rule with logical flow in core-overrides
Solution3: a11y.scss rule with aria-live for order announcements
Solution4: Theme script include for dynamic tab order management

ExpandedDescription: Some icon-only buttons (e.g. glyphicon action buttons in tables or toolbars) lack tabindex or proper role, making them unreachable by keyboard, violating WCAG 2.1.1 and 2.4.7 in lists and dashboards.
AcceptanceCriteria: Every icon-only button is keyboard-focusable and announces its purpose.
Test: Tab through all icon buttons in litmus widget; confirm each receives focus and has tooltip/aria-label.
Solution1: Add tabindex=0" and role="button" via CSS selector in core-overrides"
Solution2: CSS var + focus styles for icon buttons
Solution3: a11y.scss rule with aria-label enforcement
Solution4: Theme-level global button accessibility fix

ExpandedDescription: Dropdown menus (.dropdown-menu and custom select-like widgets) do not respond to keyboard (no arrow key navigation, no Enter to select, no proper ARIA), violating WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard and 2.4.3 Focus Order. This breaks filters, navigation menus, and catalog variable selectors for keyboard-only users.
AcceptanceCriteria: Dropdowns open with Space/Enter, allow full arrow navigation and selection, and close with Esc; all items are focusable.
Test: Tab to dropdown, open with keyboard, navigate items, select, and close; confirm full operability in litmus widget and real filter.
Solution1: Add vanilla JS keyboard event listener polyfill for dropdowns
Solution2: CSS rule with aria-haspopup and role=menu in core-overrides
Solution3: Full ARIA + keyboard support block in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme JS Include for global dropdown keyboard handler

SpecificIssue: Carousel controls not reachable by keyboard
ExpandedDescription: Carousel previous/next buttons and indicators lack focus styles or tabindex, making slide navigation impossible by keyboard, violating WCAG 2.1.1 and 2.4.7 in featured content or knowledge carousels.
AcceptanceCriteria: Carousel controls are fully keyboard-focusable, operable, and announce current slide.
Test: Tab through carousel in litmus widget, advance slides with keyboard, and confirm controls receive visible focus.
Solution1: Override .carousel-control { tabindex: 0; } + focus styles in core
Solution2: CSS vars for carousel button colors + focus ring
Solution3: Dedicated carousel keyboard rule in a11y.scss
Solution4: Portal widget override for accessible carousel

ExpandedDescription: Elements positioned off-screen (e.g. hidden side panels or modal backgrounds) remain focusable, causing keyboard focus to disappear or trap users, violating WCAG 2.4.3 and 2.4.7.
AcceptanceCriteria: All off-screen elements have tabindex=-1 or aria-hidden when not visible; focus never lands on them.
Test: Tab through page with off-screen elements open/closed; confirm focus never jumps to hidden areas.
Solution1: Add CSS rule html body [aria-hidden=true"] { display: none; } with tabindex handling in core"
Solution2: JS helper to manage tabindex dynamically
Solution3: a11y.scss rule enforcing aria-hidden on off-screen elements
Solution4: Theme script for global focus management

ExpandedDescription: Focus ring on long pages or scrollable widgets disappears when scrolling, violating WCAG 2.4.7 because keyboard users lose track of position during scrolling content.
AcceptanceCriteria: Focus indicator remains visible at all times during scroll (sticky or re-applied).
Test: Scroll a long page while focused on an element in litmus widget; confirm ring stays visible.
Solution1: Add position: sticky or will-change to focus rules in core-overrides
Solution2: CSS var for persistent focus with box-shadow fallback
Solution3: !important scroll-safe focus block in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme-level scroll event listener for focus refresh

SpecificIssue: Custom widget buttons lack focus styles
ExpandedDescription: Custom ServiceNow widgets (e.g. catalog cards, list actions) have buttons without any focus indicator due to overriding Bootstrap defaults, violating WCAG 2.4.7 and 2.4.11 across hundreds of pages.
AcceptanceCriteria: Every custom widget button receives the same high-contrast focus ring as core buttons.
Test: Inspect buttons in custom widgets via litmus or real page; tab and confirm visible focus on all.
Solution1: Add broad selector html body .widget button:focus-visible { outline... } in core
Solution2: CSS var reuse + widget class targeting
Solution3: High-specificity a11y.scss override for .widget
Solution4: Theme CSS Variables for widget button focus

ExpandedDescription: Navbar menu items (including dropdown toggles and links) are skipped during tab navigation or receive focus out of visual order due to Bootstrap float layout and hidden mobile elements, violating WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order and forcing keyboard users to miss key navigation options.
AcceptanceCriteria: All navbar items are reachable in logical visual order with no skips when tabbing.
Test: Tab through the entire navbar in litmus widget and real portal; confirm every visible item receives focus sequentially.
Solution1: Add explicit tabindex=0" and flex ordering in core-overrides for navbar"
Solution2: CSS logical properties + aria-flow in core
Solution3: Dedicated navbar tab order rule in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme/Portal navbar widget standardization

ExpandedDescription: Radio button and checkbox groups lack grouped focus styling or proper fieldset/legend association, causing individual items to have weak or missing focus rings and poor grouping announcement, violating WCAG 2.4.7 and 1.3.1.
AcceptanceCriteria: Every radio/checkbox group has a clear grouped focus indicator and visible legend.
Test: Tab through radio/checkbox sections in litmus widget; confirm visible ring on group and logical focus movement.
Solution1: Override .radio, .checkbox { outline on focus } with fieldset styling in core
Solution2: CSS vars for group focus + legend boost
Solution3: a11y.scss rule enforcing fieldset/legend
Solution4: Theme form component template update

SpecificIssue: Date picker calendar keyboard navigation fails
ExpandedDescription: Bootstrap date picker calendar (or SN date fields) does not support full keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Enter selection, Esc close), violating WCAG 2.1.1 and 2.4.7 for users entering dates in catalog items or filters.
AcceptanceCriteria: Date picker is fully keyboard operable: arrows move dates, Enter selects, Esc closes, focus returns correctly.
Test: Open date picker in litmus widget, navigate dates with keyboard only, select and close; confirm success.
Solution1: Add vanilla JS keyboard handler polyfill for date inputs
Solution2: CSS rule enabling focus on calendar cells in core
Solution3: Full ARIA + keyboard block in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme script include for date picker accessibility

SpecificIssue: Tree expander arrows not keyboard friendly
ExpandedDescription: Tree expander arrows (+/- icons) are not focusable or lack keyboard activation (Space/Enter), violating WCAG 2.1.1 in hierarchical trees such as knowledge base or service catalog folders.
AcceptanceCriteria: Every tree expander is keyboard-focusable, activates on Enter/Space, and announces expanded/collapsed state.
Test: Expand/collapse tree nodes in litmus widget using only keyboard; confirm focus and announcement work.
Solution1: Add role=button" tabindex="0" via CSS selector in core-overrides"
Solution2: CSS var + focus ring for arrows
Solution3: a11y.scss rule with aria-expanded enforcement
Solution4: Theme tree widget override

ExpandedDescription: Pagination links (.pagination li a) have weak or missing focus indicators and poor visual feedback, violating WCAG 2.4.7 and making it hard for keyboard users to know which page link is active during long result sets.
AcceptanceCriteria: All pagination links show clear high-contrast focus ring and active state distinction.
Test: Tab through pagination in litmus widget; confirm every link has visible focus.
Solution1: Override .pagination li a:focus-visible { outline: 4px solid #0066cc; } in core
Solution2: CSS vars for pagination focus
Solution3: High-contrast !important in a11y.scss
Solution4: Theme-level pagination component fix

ExpandedDescription: Images throughout ServiceNow Service Portal (logos, icons, charts, catalog item images, search result thumbnails, and widget graphics) have no alt attribute or empty alt=", violating WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content. Screen reader users hear nothing or generic filenames, losing all context and meaning.
AcceptanceCriteria: Every meaningful image has a descriptive alt attribute; decorative images have alt=" and are aria-hidden.
Test: Run axe-core/WAVE on any page with images in litmus widget or real portal; confirm zero missing alt" violations and all images are announced correctly."
Solution1: Add alt text via widget HTML templates or CSS ::after pseudo-content fallback with aria-label in core-overrides
